displayTitle: The H&D Curve: Every Film's Personality

beats.narration: Every industrial film has a personality, and one graph captures it: the characteristic curve — H and D — density against log relative exposure.

payload.title: THE H&D CURVE
payload.sub: The film's whole personality, on one graph.

beats.narration: Left to right: base plus fog, the toe where flaws hide, then a working region that keeps steepening. More density, more contrast.

payload.heading: Reading the curve, left to right

beats.narration: Speed is position: fast films sit left, slow films right. Read the gap where they cross density two. Grain size buys speed — and costs sharpness.

payload.heading: Speed is position on the curve

beats.narration: Standards group films into classes: special and class one, finest and slowest; class two, the everyday workhorse; class three, coarser and faster.
So the rule for choosing: pick the fastest film that still proves the required image quality indicator — then write it into the technique.

payload.sub: Then write it into the technique sheet.
